coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of graphene quantum dots by laser-induced polymer
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jinping
Qin, Yue
Li, Xin
Wang, Ruirong
Guo, Hao
Tang, Jun
Liu, Jun
Liu, Lishuang
coredata.dc:description: In order to achieve a low-cost, simple, fast, and green preparation method of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a method for one-step preparation of GQDs by laser-induced polyimide powder was proposed. By controlling the laser parameters and using low-cost precursor material polyimide (PI) powder, a bright blue fluorescent GQDs solution was successfully prepared, and the whole experimental process was simulated by ReaxFF molecular reaction dynamics. The results show that the GQDs solution with blue fluorescent can be prepared by laser-induced PI power in one step. The scraped black matter contained GQDs. After the samples were tested by UV–vis, PL, TEM, and Raman, the prepared GQDs had an average particle size of 6.14 nm, a quantum yield of about 11.4%, and stable fluorescence properties. The whole process of the proposed method can be carried out at ambient temperature without any other chemical reagents or complicated subsequent processing, and has the advantages of simplicity, rapidity, environmental protection, and low cost, which not only provides a new reference for the green synthesis of quantum, but also has the potential for large-scale production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyimide
Laser-induced
One-step preparation
Graphene quantum dots
ReaxFF

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the synthesis of GQDs.
(a) Surface topography of Raman test site (red box); (b) Raman spectra of unstripped precursor samples.

(a)UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the GQDs, Inset: Photograph of the GQDs solution taken under visible light and 365 nm UV irradiation; (b) Photoluminescence spectra of the GQDs (Under 365 nm excitation light); (c) Photostability test of GQDs solution under 365 nm UV light.
(a) TEM image of GQDs (Right: HRTEM image); (b) Size distribution histogram of GQDs.

(a) Graphene structure at the end of ReaxFF simulation at 2900 K (right: different types of rings (5-, 6-, and 7-member rings) formed, highlighted by the red box in left); (b) Products of each element.
Hydrogen functional group edge structure modification.
Modification of edge structure of oxygen-containing functional group.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of graphene quantum dots by laser-induced polymer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Instrumentation Science and Dynamic Measurement, School of Instrument and Electronics, North University of China, Taiyuan, 030051, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Instrumentation Science and Dynamic Measurement

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Instrumentation Science and Dynamic Measurement. School of Instrument and Electronics, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to achieve a low-cost, simple, fast, and green preparation method of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a method for one-step preparation of GQDs by laser-induced polyimide powder was proposed. By controlling the laser parameters and using low-cost precursor material polyimide (PI) powder, a bright blue fluorescent GQDs solution was successfully prepared, and the whole experimental process was simulated by ReaxFF molecular reaction dynamics. The results show that the GQDs solution with blue fluorescent can be prepared by laser-induced PI power in one step. The scraped black matter contained GQDs. After the samples were tested by UV–vis, PL, TEM, and Raman, the prepared GQDs had an average particle size of 6.14 nm, a quantum yield of about 11.4%, and stable fluorescence properties. The whole process of the proposed method can be carried out at ambient temperature without any other chemical reagents or complicated subsequent processing, and has the advantages of simplicity, rapidity, environmental protection, and low cost, which not only provides a new reference for the green synthesis of quantum, but also has the potential for large-scale production.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are small fragments with a transverse size of less than 100 nm and a few-layer (1–10) graphene structure [1–3]. Compared with traditional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [4,5] (e.g. ZnS, TiO, CdX, X = S, Se, Te), GQDs have excellent properties such as stable fluorescence, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility [6–9] due to prominent quantum confinement effect of conjugated π-domains and edge effects [10,11]. Therefore, it has shown great potential applications in sensors, heavy metal ion detection, biomedicine, energy application, and other fields [12–17].

The synthesis method of GQDs is one of the most concerned directions in the research of GQDs and has considerable research significance. In recent years, many approaches have been explored to generate GQDs, which are mainly divided into two categories: “top-down” and “bottom-up” methods [18]. The top-down approaches usually cut larger carbon structures materials (graphite, graphene, graphene oxide, coal, carbon nanotubes, etc.) into appropriately sized GQDs via physical or chemical approaches, for instance, oxidative cutting, electrochemical exfoliation, and microwave or ultrasonic breaking [19,20]. In contrast, the bottom-up approaches usually fuse small organic materials (citric acid, glucose, etc.) into GQDs of suitable size by pyrolysis or carbonization, such as stepwise organic synthesis, decomposition of fulleren C60, pyrolysis of precursors materials [21,22]. Although some approaches for preparing GQDs have the advantages of simplicity and high yield, they also exist some disadvantages, such as costly precursor materials and reactor, complex preparation process, long preparation procedure, and highly toxic or corrosive chemicals (e.g. H2SO4, HNO3, NaOH) [23–25]. Therefore, developing an easy, environmentally friendly, inexpensive, and rapid method to synthesize GQDs has been a hot topic for researchers, and it is full of challenges.

With the continuous development of laser science, laser induction has become a valuable tool for rapid, inexpensive and non-contact processing of materials. In terms of graphene preparation, Lin et al. proposed laser-induced graphene (LIG), that is, using the photothermal effect caused by a CO2 infrared laser under ambient conditions. Three-dimensional porous graphene is prepared on the surface of the polyimide (PI) film [26]. This method has the advantages of simple, low cost, no catalyst, and abundant precursors. Research has found that any laser that provides energy exceeding the threshold value of the material can be used to prepare LIG [27]. In terms of the preparation of GQDs, Zhang et al. proposed an ultrafast and low-cost method for GQDs. By controlling the laser output power and scanning rate, GQDs with the size of 4.5 nm and stable blue fluorescence were prepared on graphite paper, the yield of GQDs can reach about 31.458 mg/s, which shows promising potential for large-scale production [28]. In addition, the edges of most GQDs are modified by oxygen-containing functional groups to cause luminescence, but Zhu et al. proposed defective single-layer graphene prepared by chemical vapor deposition (CVD) as a precursor material. Through strong ultrasonic pulverization, the defective graphene is cut into small-size blue and stable fluorescent GQDs. The oxygen-containing functional groups on the edges of the quantum dots can be ignored, and the main reason for luminescence is the zigzag edge structure [29].

Therefore, on this basis, we propose a one-step method for the synthesis of GQDs by the laser-induced polymer. In this method, polyimide (PI) powder is uniformly dispersed on the surface of polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) as the precursor material, and a stable, bright blue fluorescent GQDs solution is prepared by the one-step method. At the same time, the samples were characterized and analyzed by UV absorption spectrum, Fluorescence spectrum, Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Raman spectrum. Furthermore, the whole experimental process was simulated and verified by ReaxFF molecular reaction dynamics. In general, compared with the complex procedures and chemical pollution required by other existing methods, this method is simple, fast and has superior advantages, which provides a new reference for the simple and green preparation of GQDs.

In this experiment, ultrafine PI powder was used as the precursor material. Since PI powder is easy to agglomerate, we chose the flexible silicone material Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) as the substrate. PDMS was selected as a transparent flexible substrate. The main reasons are as follows: 1) PDMS is composed of main agent and curing agent, and the surface of PDMS can be viscous by controlling the curing time. The PI powder can be uniformly dispersed on the surface of PDMS to make it stable and fully react under laser induction. In addition, the cured PDMS can be cut to any size. 2) PDMS has favourable tensile flexibility, which makes the prepared products easier to peel off.

PDMS selected American Dow Corning SYLGARD 184 silicone rubber, its prepolymer and a curing agent were manually mixed in the ratio of 10:1 (5 ml: 0.5 ml) in a beaker and was stirred by the glass rod for 5 min to make the mixture uniform. Then the mixture was poured into the petri dish, and the bubbles caused by stirring were removed by vacuum. The PDMS with a thickness of about 2 mm was prepared by curing at 60 °C for 30 min. Polyimide (PI) powder was selected from the thermoplastic PI powder of DuPont Company in the United States, and used as the original sample, uniformly dispersed on the PDMS surface with a thickness of about 0.1 mm. The material prepared above was cut into small pieces with a size of 1 cm × 3 cm and put on the glass slide. The ethanol solution (95% analytically pure) used in the experiment was purchased from Guangzhou Kema reagent.

The schematic diagram of laser-induced polymer to generate GQDs is shown in Fig. 1 . Firstly, a 532 nm green laser (MW-GL-532, Maximum power 2 W, Beijing Changfu Technology) was used to perform laser ablation, the 1.0 W of laser power and the 1 mm/s of scanning speed were selected to irradiate the sample to prepare GQDs. The laser was focused on the surface of the sample with a 20 × objective lens. The glass slide with the precursor material is placed on the electric displacement table, and the electric displacement table was controlled by computer to realize laser scanning. The scanning length of the electric displacement table from top to bottom was 1 cm. After 2 times of scanning repetition, the displacement table moves 1 mm to the right, and the process was repeated continuously to form a large area of black substance.

Secondly, the scraped black powder was poured into a beaker containing 10 ml of ethanol solution, and then ultrasonically dispersed for 30 min by an ultrasonic cell pulverizer (Ningbo Xinzhi Biotechnology Co., Ltd., Model JY96-I IN.).

Finally, the GQDs solution was obtained by removing the large impurities in the solution through filtration or by standing to collect the supernatant. GQDs was characterized by ultraviolet absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra, TEM and Raman spectroscopy. All experiments were carried out under environmental conditions.

Raman spectroscopy was obtained using a Renishaw spectrometer excited by a 514 nm laser, focused through a 50 × microscope objective. The ultraviolet-absorption spectrum and the fluorescence spectrum were respectively used Shimadzu ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer and Edinburgh steady/transient fluorescence spectrophotometer FLS100. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were obtained from JEM 2100F (Japan, JEOL) operating at 200 kV accelerating voltage. All characterizations in this experiment were under normal temperature environmental conditions.

Raman spectroscopy is an effective means to characterize carbon substances. After the laser irradiation, the pristine PI powder used changes from yellow to black. Fig. 2 (a) is an optical microscope image of a black line under a 20 × objective lens. Fig. 2(b) shows the Raman spectrum of the test site. There are three typical characteristic peaks in the Raman spectrum, which are the D peak located at ∼1353 cm−1, G peak at ∼1581 cm−1, and 2D peak at ∼2707 cm−1. The D peak reflects the degree of structural defects or disordered arrangement in carbon materials, which is the characteristic peak of defects. The G peak reflects the symmetry and crystallinity of the sample, which is the characteristic peak of the sp2 hybrid structure. The 2D peak is caused by the two-phonon resonant transition scattering and reflects the layer thickness of graphene, which is also the basis for judging the formation of graphene. In addition, the relative strength ratio of the D peak and G peak (ID/IG) is used to reflect the defect degree of the material. Using the relative strength ratio of 2D peak to G peak (I2D/IG) to reflect the number of graphene layers (When the ratio of 2D peak to G peak is greater than 1, it is monolayer graphene) [30]. According to the figure, ID/IG = 0.423, I2D/IG = 0.912, the G and 2D peaks are separate peaks. Therefore, it can be explained that the black material prepared by laser on the PI surface is a defective and well-crystallized few-layer (<5) graphene structure. The black material is peeled off and dispersed by ultrasound, which also shows that the number of layers of GQDs that we will generate later can only be less than this.

GQDs have excellent fluorescence properties. Fig. 3 (a) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQDs, in which the inset shows photographs of GQDs solutions under visible light and 365 nm ultraviolet light. As can be seen from the inset, the GQDs solution is transparent under visible light, while a bright blue fluorescence is observed under ultraviolet light. Furthermore, as can be seen from the spectra curve (Fig. 3(a)), the sample has obvious absorption values in the range of 200 nm in the deep ultraviolet region to 600 nm in the visible light region. There is an obvious absorption peak at 209 nm, which is attributed to the π-π* electronic transition of aromatic CC bonds, indicating the graphene structure of GQDs. And GQDs appear a weak absorption peak at 298 nm, which can be attributed to the n-π* electron transition of CO bonds [31]. Fig. 3(b) shows the photoluminescence spectra of GQDs under UV excitation at 365 nm. It can be seen that GQDs has a strong fluorescence peak at 473 nm, which verifies the fluorescence characteristics of the sample solution. Meanwhile, we also tested the fluorescence stability of the prepared quantum dots for a period of 24 days (every two days). As shown in Fig. 3(c), the fluorescence intensity of GQDs is basically remained stable, indicating that the prepared GQDs has good fluorescence stability.

The quantum yield of GQDs was calculated by formula 1 with quinine sulfate as the standard reference.Quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 having a quantum yield of 0.54 [32]. In order to avoid the influence of reabsorption effect, the absorbance value of quinine sulfate solution and the sample to be tested should be strictly controlled within the range of less than 0.1. The quantum yield calculation formula is as follows:
(1)
Q
Y 2
= Q
Y 1
(
A 1
A 2
)
(
F 2
F 1
)
(
n 2
n 1
)
2
Where, QY1 and QY2 are the fluorescence quantum yield of the standard reference and the sample, A1 and A2 are the integral area of the fluorescence spectrum of the standard reference and the sample, F1 and F2 are the absorbance values and n1 and n2 are the refractive index of the reference and the sample solution, respectively. Since the concentration of quinine sulfate and the sample to be tested are relatively small, there is little difference in refraction rate between them. Therefore, this paper sets the refraction rate of quinine sulfate solution and the sample to be tested to be the same. The fluorescence quantum yield of the prepared GQDs was calculated to be about 11.4%, which is much larger than that in Ref. [33].

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to test the morphology and size of GQDs. TEM image of the prepared GQDs is shown in Fig. 4 (a). It can be observed that GQDs is mainly round and uniformly dispersed. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of GQDs in inset exhibit the well-resolved lattice stripes. The measured lattice spacing is 0.24 nm, which matches the graphene (1120) crystal plane, indicating that the prepared GQDs has high crystallinity. Fig. 4(b) shows the particle size distribution of GQDs. It can be concluded that the particle size distribution of GQDs ranges from ∼3 to ∼9 nm. The average particle size of ∼6.14 nm.

The graphene structure of GQDs was investigated by Raman spectroscopy. Fig. 5 is the Raman spectra of GQDs. The D peak appears at ∼1342 cm−1, the G peak appears at ∼1585 cm−1, and the 2D peak appears at ∼2700 cm−1. The relative intensity ratio of the 2D peak to the G peak (I2D/IG), is 0.405. The relative intensity ratio of the D peak to G peak (ID/IG) is about 0.865. The prominent D and G peaks and strong 2D peak indicate that the prepared GQDs have higher defects and better crystallinity.

The molecular reaction dynamics method combines molecular dynamics with a chemical reaction force field that can be described as a bond-breaking process. The ReaxFF reactive force field [34] is a computational simulation method between quantization simulation and molecular dynamics simulation, which does not need to fix the continuity between atoms in the molecule. Under the appropriate computational force field and reaction temperature, the chemical bonds between each atom can be broken and formed freely during the simulation process, which is used to simulate the chemical reaction of the molecular system. ReaxFF simulation module has a rich selection of force fields, easy to operate, and can see the whole process of bonds breaking and forming dynamically and the changes in the content of various products, which provides a possible solution to explore the mechanism of GQDs preparation from the molecular level.

The process of forming GQDs by laser irradiation on polymers is based on the photothermal effect. When the laser acts on the precursor material, the energy generated by the laser will cause the lattice vibration of the irradiated area, leading to localized heating. Under such a high temperature, the chemical bonds in the precursor could easily break. Subsequently, the carbon atoms will be rearranged to form a graphene structure, and the remaining atoms will be recombined and released in the form of gas. Finally, in this process, sp3 carbon atoms are transformed into sp2 lattice under the irradiation of laser [27,35]. In this study, we choose the ReaxFF reaction force field simulation module in Feimi Technology Amsterdam Modeling Suite (AMS) software (version 2020.102) to simulated and analyze the whole experimental process. The simulated molecular structure of PI material is shown in Fig. 6 , which consists of four elements: C, H, O, and N.

To get a balanced, homogeneous reactant and a reliable simulation structure, it is necessary to relax the whole system at room temperature before using ReaxFF for formal molecular dynamics simulation. The designated product is removed during the reaction process, which is used to simulate the discharge of the generated gas.

For the initial system, 35 PI molecules were introduced into a 30 × 30 × 30 Å cube box, the time step of 0.25 fs was chosen here, and the force field selection CHON-2019.ff. Firstly, the relaxation reaction was carried out at a room temperature of 300 K, and the total step was set to 30,000 steps. Then, the system after relaxation was taken as the initial system, the simulation temperature was set at 2900 K and the total step of reactive process was set to 1,000,000 steps. Finally, the simulation results of each time were put into the new initial system, the parameters were kept unchanged and the process was repeated three times.

As shown in Fig. 7 (a), the final simulation results showed that the PI material could form a graphene structure at the temperature of 2900 K, which contains 5-, 6-, and 7-member carbon rings, the five-membered and seven-membered carbon rings are few, and the six-membered carbon rings are the main ones. The oxygen has been ejected entirely as a gas, partly doped with nitrogen atoms. In addition, the intact graphene sheets are all composed of hexagonal carbon atoms. When there are pentagonal and heptagonal defects or other local defects, the defect D peak in the Raman spectrum is generated. This is consistent with the observation in references [27,36].

The various elements in the PI material are tracked, as shown in Fig. 7(b). It can be seen that the oxygen element in the PI material is mainly released in the form of CO, but part of it also exists in CO2, H2O, and disappears completely after the simulation ends. The hydrogen element is mainly released in the form of H2, but part of it is also present in H2O and CXHYNZ (e.g.C2H4, C2H2, C2H3), and the nitrogen element mainly exists in the form of CXHYNZ gas, such as CHN, C2HN, C2HN.

As shown in Fig. 8 , it can be seen that the edge of graphene is modified mainly by hydrogen functional groups in the simulation results. The oxygen element has been completely released in the form of gas, and it is almost not contained at the edges. Subsequently, the graphene sheet structure formed by the final simulation results is taken as the initial simulation system of the next simulation, and a small amount of –OH group is added to the system. After relaxation, the simulation is carried out under normal temperature conditions (400 K). The results are shown in Fig. 9 . It can be found some of the –OH groups can be well modified on the edges of the graphene structure and the carbon chains of the edge structure. This process is used to simulate the ultrasonic dispersion of the prepared black sample powder in ethanol solution.

Many factors could cause the luminescence of GQDs. At present, several mechanisms have been proposed to explain the reasons for luminescence. Without considering the influence of the external environment, the main factors causing the luminescence of GQDs include the size, shape [37], edge type (zigzag, armchair shape) [38], surface functional groups (including type, content, and location) [39], and defect effects [40]. Combined with the simulation results, it can be found that the graphene structure contains five-, six-, seven-membered ring structures, and some N atoms are doped in, which leads to the defects of graphene. Since no reagent was added during the whole experiment, the oxygen-containing functional groups in ethanol solution are not enough to modify the edge structure, so they can be ignored. Therefore, it is speculated that the blue fluorescent GQDs are mainly caused by structural defects.

In summary, we report a rapid and easy to operate laser-induced method to produce GQDs. Using PI powder evenly dispersed on the surface of PDMS as the precursor material, the bright blue fluorescence GQDs solution is successfully obtained by controlling the laser parameters. The prepared GQDs have an average particle size of 6.14 nm and a quantum yield of about 11.4%. They are easily dispersed in ethanol solution and have stable fluorescence properties. ReaxFF molecular reaction dynamics was used to simulate the whole experiment process, The results show that PI can form graphene structure at the suitable simulated temperature, in which elements other than carbon are recombined and released in the form of gas, and the carbon atoms are connected to form graphene structure. This structure is dominated by six-membered carbon rings, and also contains five-membered and seven-membered carbon rings, in which a small amount of nitrogen atoms are doped, and hydrogen atoms are mainly modified on the edge structure. The proposed laser-induced PI one-step preparation of GQDs has the following advantages: 1) the precursor materials are low-cost and easy to obtain; 2) the operation is simple and does not require harsh experimental conditions; 3) no environmental pollution because none of chemical reagents such as strong acids and alkali, avoiding any complicated post-processes. This work not only provides a new reference for the preparation of green and low-cost GQDs, but also has the potential for large-scale production.

Jinping Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yue Qin: Software, Investigation. Xin Li: Supervision. Ruirong Wang: Supervision. Hao Guo: Validation, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Jun Tang: Validation, Supervision. Jun Liu: Validation, Supervision. Lishuang Liu: Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No.51775522), Shanxi province key laboratory of quantum sensing and precision measurement (Grant No.201905D121001), Shanxi ‘1331 Project’ Key Subjects Construction, Applied Basic Research Program in Shanxi Province (201901D211203, 201801D221230).
